evals.name: check-only-stops-short
evals.prompt: Is upstream MiniMax ahead of my pinned baseline? Just want to know — don't apply anything yet.
evals.expected_output: Reads ~/.dot/nvim/CLAUDE.md to find the baseline commit and variant. Calls `gh api repos/nvim-mini/MiniMax/commits/HEAD` for the new SHA and `compare/<baseline>...HEAD` for the diff. Reports a summary table (commits ahead, files touching the user's variant, notable commit messages). Stops there — does NOT overlay or modify nvim/. Does NOT confuse this with `update-nvim-plugins` (that's a different skill for lockfile bumps).

evals.name: lockfile-is-not-overlaid
evals.prompt: Pull the latest MiniMax baseline into my nvim config and reapply my deviations.
evals.expected_output: Carries out the full overlay workflow but EXCLUDES `nvim-pack-lock.json` from both the fetch and the copy — that file is managed by `update-nvim-plugins`, not by upstream MiniMax. Verifies the working tree is clean before overlaying. Runs the step-4 sanity diff (baseline temp dir vs current nvim/) to confirm no undocumented deviations exist; if any are found, stops and surfaces them rather than silently overwriting. Reapplies the deviations recorded in CLAUDE.md and advances the baseline pointer in CLAUDE.md. (Baseline failure mode: blindly copying the entire `configs/nvim-0.13/` tree including the lockfile, clobbering the user's plugin pins.)

evals.name: deviation-conflict-surfaces
evals.prompt: Run the MiniMax update. I know upstream refactored the tree-sitter block recently and I'm worried it'll clash with my NVIM_NO_EXTERNAL_TREESITTER guard.
evals.expected_output: Recognizes this as the deviation-conflict edge case. After overlay, attempts to reapply the env-guard wrap; when it doesn't have a clean anchor (upstream's new shape doesn't match the wrap's preconditions), STOPS and surfaces both sides — the upstream diff and the recorded deviation — and asks the user how to resolve (re-author the deviation, drop it if upstream now covers the case natively, or abort the bump). Does NOT silently merge or pick one side. (Baseline failure mode: applying the deviation against the wrong block, or silently dropping it because the pattern didn't match.)
